statusLine: EU fuel security remains under strain with 15 of 27 countries holding critical stock levels in at least one fuel type, though overall reserves narrowly exceed mandatory minimums.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security shows mixed signals in January 2026, with EU-wide averages of 94.5 days for petrol, 95.8 days for diesel, and 89.9 days for jet fuel. While petrol and diesel reserves marginally exceed the 90-day mandatory minimum, jet fuel stocks have fallen below this threshold at 89.9 days, indicating tightening aviation fuel supplies across the bloc.

The distribution of reserves reveals significant regional disparities and vulnerabilities. Romania faces the most severe shortages with all three fuel types in critical status, including alarmingly low diesel reserves at just 20.2 days. Poland, Spain, and Luxembourg also show critical levels across all fuel categories. In total, 15 of 27 EU countries have at least one fuel type at critical levels, while only 7 countries maintain safe status across all three fuel types.

Jet fuel emerges as the most constrained fuel type, with 18 of 27 countries showing critical or warning levels. This reflects ongoing pressure on aviation fuel supplies amid Middle East disruptions. The recent strategic reserve releases and Brent crude's 27% price increase since the conflict began underscore the challenging supply environment, with current oil prices at $95.2 per barrel contributing to elevated fuel costs across member states.
keyPoints: 15 of 27 EU countries have critical stock levels in at least one fuel type, with Romania showing the most severe shortages across all categories
Jet fuel stocks have fallen below the 90-day mandatory minimum at 89.9 days EU-wide, with 18 of 27 countries at critical or warning levels
Only 7 of 27 countries maintain safe reserves across all three fuel types: Sweden, Ireland, Malta, Finland, Slovenia, Estonia, and Hungary
Regional disparities are stark, with Eastern European countries particularly affected - Romania's diesel reserves at just 20.2 days represent the lowest level
Brent crude at $95.2 per barrel reflects a 27% increase since Middle East disruptions began, contributing to elevated fuel prices across the EU
